phrasal_verbs.meaning: start to have more colour or light; give something more colour or light; start looking or feeling happier; if the weather brightens up, it becomes sunnier
practise and improve your skills or knowledge of something
examine someone or something in order to be certain that everything is correct, true or satisfactory; if information checks out, you feel that it is true after examining it
make something more interesting or exciting, or become more interesting or exciting; give something a more interesting appearance, taste or other quality
change or improve the appearance of someone or something; makeover (n)
make a mistake or do something badly; make something dirty or untidy; be the cause of someone’s physical, emotional or mental problems
hide a problem or disagreement rather than finding a satisfactory solution to it
repair something, often quickly and not very well; become friends with someone again after a disagreement; give basic medical treatment to someone who is injured
if an organisation or area is run down, its size, importance and activity is reduced; run-down (adj)
succeed in doing something, but not in a very impressive way
improve the appearance of something, for example by cleaning or painting it; if you smarten up or smarten yourself up, you make yourself look tidy and clean
be much more impressive or important than others; be easy to see or notice because of being different; outstanding (adj)
make a surface look better with small improvements
gradually become thinner and weaker over a period of time, usually because of an illness
damage a vehicle so badly that it is not worth repairing; decide that someone or something will not succeed and stop giving them your attention and energy; write-off (n)

art: have/get sth down to a fine art
best: to the best of my knowledge
to the best of sb’s ability
bottom: from the bottom of my heart
top: sth gets on top of you
off the top of your head

idioms.meaning: someone or something that you are very pleased to see
a weak feature of someone or something that could cause failure or be attacked
below the usual or expected standard
completely honest or legal; extremely clean
say that you will definitely not allow or accept something
the newest and best type of something
more than what is considered normal or suitable
in the place that is most central or important
receive a lot of attention because you give the best performance in a show or other event
an advantage that makes someone or something more successful than other people or things

A.title: Choose the correct word. If both options are correct, choose both.
A.instructions: Choose the correct word. If both options are correct, choose both.

questions.sentence: The software works fine, but we could **better / streamline** it further to reduce the loading time.
The hotel lobby has been completely **renovated / refurbished**, though the guest rooms are still waiting for attention.
Spreading those rumours is only **exacerbating / aggravating** the tension between the two departments.
The committee will be **evaluating / valuing** each candidate’s presentation before making a final decision.
A burst pipe at the industrial plant has **decayed / contaminated** the groundwater in the surrounding area.
She set the regional marathon record five years ago and it has never been **strengthened / bettered** since.
Adding carefully chosen background music can really **enhance / reinforce** the atmosphere of a dining experience.
High street sales have **declined / devastated** significantly since the out-of-town retail park opened.
Economists are warning that unemployment must not **surpass / outclass** the five per cent threshold.
The injury has completely **wrecked / worsened** her chances of competing in next month’s championship.
Overusing the term ‘genius’ has **cheapened / worsened** it to the point where it carries almost no weight.
The ticket launch was meant to go smoothly, but the whole thing turned into a complete **chaos / shambles**.
The sculpture is almost perfect, apart from a slight **blemish / flaw** near the base.

B.title: Select the word that best matches each statement.
B.instructions: Select the word that best matches each statement.

questions.sentence: ‘The detail on this porcelain vase is absolutely breathtaking.’ **exquisite / sound**
‘The film was dreadful, but at least the soundtrack gave it one positive element.’ **redeeming / ultimate**
‘The old gate’s hinges have turned a deep shade of brown.’ **stale / rusty**
‘What they’ve offered us really doesn’t meet our expectations.’ **invaluable / inadequate**
‘The stitching on this jacket fell apart after the very first wash.’ **detrimental / shoddy**
‘The screen on my new laptop has stopped responding altogether.’ **defective / detrimental**
‘This piece of fruit has gone completely dark and soft inside.’ **rotten / stale**
‘This turned out to be the leading cause of the project’s failure.’ **optimum / prime**
‘We simply couldn’t have finished the report without your contribution.’ **exquisite / invaluable**
‘The rent isn’t too bad for the size of the flat. I’ll take it.’ **ultimate / satisfactory**
‘This loaf has been sitting out for a few days and has lost its freshness.’ **rotten / stale**
‘The proposed cuts will be seriously harmful to local services.’ **detrimental / defective**
‘Nothing will ever compare to the thrill of skydiving for the very first time!’ **ultimate / optimum**
‘She put forward a very well-reasoned argument in the debate.’ **prime / sound**
‘This location has absolutely everything we were looking for.’ **ideal / prime**
‘The performance last night was exceptional from start to finish.’ **satisfactory / first-rate**
‘Five turns out to be just the right number of people for this working group.’ **ultimate / optimum**

C.title: Write a word from the boxes in each gap.
C.instructions: Write a word from the boxes in each gap.

questions.sentence: My colleague Antonio was absolutely raving about Patricia Ortega’s new novel. Apparently it became a (1) ...... almost overnight after its release.
I’m holding off buying it myself – I’d rather wait for it to come out in (2) ...... before spending that much.
Rehearsals for our amateur production are going well. I’ve memorised all my (3) ...... without much difficulty, though I still struggle to remember the (4) ...... to the songs we perform at the end.
I’m thinking of attending the piano (5) ...... at the arts centre tomorrow evening. The featured composer wrote the (6) ...... for two award-winning films, which makes it even more interesting.
My cousin took me to a (7) ...... of Nina Valdés’ paintings at the national gallery last weekend. The (8) ...... gave a fascinating talk about how the exhibition had been selected and organised.
Apparently, one of the pieces had been found at a flea market by someone who thought it was (9) ...... and barely worth keeping. It later turned out to be completely (10) ...... !
Among the (11) ...... I’d find most fulfilling would be helping to track down stolen (12) ...... of art and returning them to their rightful owners.

D.title: Complete using the words in the box in the correct form.
D.instructions: Complete using the words in the box in the correct form. There is one word you will not need.

questions.sentence: When art lovers think about expanding a personal collection, they often dream of owning iconic (1) ...... – such as Vermeer’s Girl with a Pearl Earring – or striking modern (2) ...... that transform entire gallery spaces.
One popular way to acquire pieces is to attend a/an (3) ...... , where both established and emerging artworks go to the highest bidder.

On any given day, a sale catalogue might list rough (4) ...... in charcoal or pen, examples of (5) ...... art spanning ceramics and metalwork, collector’s (6) ...... ranging from signed photographs to vintage posters, (7) ...... compositions that challenge the viewer’s interpretation, and even (8) ...... pieces of furniture crafted in a specific historical style.

E.title: Write one word in each gap so the second sentence has a similar meaning to the first sentence.
E.instructions: Write one word in each gap so the second sentence has a similar meaning to the first sentence.

questions.sentence:
You really need to review your watercolour technique before entering the national landscape competition.
You really need to ……………… up on your watercolour technique before entering the national landscape competition.

The van was so badly damaged in the collision that it had to be destroyed.
The van was so badly damaged in the collision that it had to be ………… off.

One piece in the exhibition is particularly remarkable for its extraordinary use of light.
One piece in the exhibition particularly ………………… out for its extraordinary use of light.

Unless audience numbers improve, the dance company will have to cancel the upcoming tour.
Unless audience numbers ………………… up, the dance company will have to cancel the upcoming tour.

I’ve heard there’s a new photography gallery near the waterfront, so I’m going to see what it’s like.
I’ve heard there’s a new photography gallery near the waterfront, so I’m going ………………… it out.

Valentina only just passed her music theory module.
Valentina ………………… through her music theory module.

The print has some faded sections, so I’ll scan it and improve it with specialist software.
The print has some faded sections, so I’ll scan it and ………………… it up with specialist software.

On tonight’s show, we’ll be completely transforming the Hendersons’ home recording studio.
On tonight’s show, we’ll be completely ………………… over the Hendersons’ home recording studio.

F.title: If a word is in the wrong sentence, write the correct word on the line. If it is correct, put OK.

questions.sentence: The studio session started slowly, but the whole atmosphere **livened** up once the lead guitarist arrived.
I’m not at all happy with this sketch – I think I’ve completely **papered** up the proportions.
I’ve only **wasted** the chair leg together for now – I won’t be able to do a proper repair until I’m back in the workshop.
The two directors have **messed** over their creative differences for the sake of getting the project finished on time.
The city council admitted that the public arts programme had been deliberately **patched** down over the past decade.
Fortunately, the skies **brightened** up just as the outdoor concert was about to begin.
We always **ran** ourselves up before attending my aunt’s gallery openings.
After the illness, he had barely eaten for three weeks and had almost **smartened** away.
Please sit down and stop interrupting. Did I **make / do** myself clear?
Come inside! It’s **nice / good** and cosy in here – you must be absolutely freezing.
Off the top of my **brain / head**, I’d say this portrait dates back to around 1750.
You’re cutting it extremely **fine / well**. The curtain goes up in about two minutes.
To the **top / best** of my knowledge, no original Monet has ever sold for under a million.
She’s just bought a **brand / model** new violin and it already sounds incredible.
Marco is his own worst **friend / enemy** – he’s incredibly talented but refuses to show his work to anyone.
There has been bad **blood / mood** between the two critics ever since that infamous review was published.
I refuse to do the director’s dirty **job / work** for her by covering up the budget discrepancies.
With decades of experience, she has the whole restoration process **round / down** to a fine art.

I.title: Complete the text with the words from the box.
I.instructions: Complete the text with the words from the box.

questions.sentence: Many people hold the view that musical standards have deteriorated and become (1) .................... over the past hundred years or so, and that most commercial music today is genuinely (2) .................... a bad way. So how do we get to the (3) .................... of this supposed contradiction? Those new (4) .................... experimental music should perhaps be forgiven for struggling to appreciate it. What needs to be (5) .................... clear is that experimental music sits at the very cutting edge of creative expression. If we dismissed all artistic risk, we would end up with the (6) .................... of all worlds – an art form completely trapped in its own past. Admittedly, some experimental music is of genuinely poor (7) ...................., but that has always been true of every art (8) .................... across every era. Yet those (9) .................... the very top of this genre are producing work that is genuinely bold and challenging. At (10) .................... finest, experimental music is as thrilling and transformative as any art can be. There is, of course, a (11) .................... line between genuine innovation and self-indulgent noise.
questions.answer: worse, in, bottom, to, made, best, quality, form, at, its, fine

K.title: Use the word given in capitals to form a word that fits in the space.
K.instructions: Use the word given in capitals to form a word that fits in the space.

questions.sentence: My uncle studied ……………………………… at Cambridge, which explains why he quotes Virgil at every family dinner. **CLASS**
David Bowie’s ability to constantly reinvent himself was truly …………………………………… – no other artist has managed anything quite like it. **IMITATE**
The film explores the themes of conflict and the …………………………………… it brings to both communities and landscapes. **DESTROY**
I have tremendous …………………………………… for anyone who dedicates their life to restoring endangered works of art. **ADMIRE**
Young audiences are particularly …………………………………… , which is precisely why the content of children’s television matters so much. **IMPRESS**
Being a …………………………………… means she spends weeks refining a single photograph before she is satisfied with it. **PERFECT**
Perhaps I’m an …………………………………… , but I firmly believe great art should be accessible to everyone, regardless of income. **IDEAL**
Many stand-up comedians have spoken openly about deep-rooted feelings of …………………………………… that first drove them to perform. **ADEQUATE**
I wasn’t entirely sure whether it would be …………………………………… to attend the symposium, but the keynote speech alone made the trip worthwhile. **WORTH**
We took the painting to a specialist for a formal …………………………………… before deciding whether to put it up for sale. **VALUE**
Now that the government has …………………………………… the wartime files, historians can finally piece together what really happened. **CLASS**
Conducting a live symphony orchestra in a world-famous concert hall is an …………………………………… responsibility. **AWE**
With her decades of experience in the theatre world, Helena is a truly …………………………………… candidate for the role of artistic director. **WORTH**
The excavation team uncovered several clay …………………………………… dating from the second century CE. **ART**
Stepping out onto the stage in front of five thousand people for the very first time was the most …………………………………… moment of my entire career. **TERROR**
The festival organisers replaced all single-use cups with ...... alternatives to cut down significantly on waste. **USE**
There is a clear ...... difference between a musician who has trained formally and one who has not. **QUALITY**

L.title: Word formation in context.
L.instructions: Complete the text with the correct form of the word in capitals.
L.passage: This week we speak to Maya Thornton, who buys and sells vintage musical instruments through online marketplaces. Before platforms like these existed, I had to trawl through hundreds of (1) ...... (CLASS) listings in specialist music magazines and drive to flea markets at weekends. These online sites are genuinely (2) ...... (IMPRESS) and a real (3) ...... (IMPROVE) on the old way of doing things. It’s quite (4) ...... (TERROR) what you can track down with a simple search! I mainly deal in (5) ...... (COLLECT) instruments from the twentieth century. These range from vintage guitars to old brass instruments to (6) ...... (ART) stage props from touring productions. (7) ...... (COLLECT) have wonderfully eclectic tastes – and there is always a buyer somewhere. The best returns come from instruments in excellent condition – often those that are (8) ...... (USE) and still in their original cases – but there is also strong demand for older pieces that might have minor (9) ...... (PERFECT) giving them added character. One of my key (10) ...... (STRONG) is the excellent reputation I have built up across these platforms; buyers trust me and return again and again. I am also skilled at identifying what is genuinely (11) ...... (VALUE) and what is frankly (12) ...... (WORTH). Here is an example: I recently found a pair of (13) ...... (MATCH) silver cufflinks being sold as genuine antiques. One look and I could tell immediately they were cheap (14) ...... (IMITATE). Real silver is essentially (15) ...... (DESTROY), and these showed obvious signs of wear. Not a purchase I would make. Ideally, it is best to inspect the (16) ...... (GOOD) in person before committing to a purchase, but return policies on these sites are generally very robust, and I have never had a serious dispute.

M.title: The prefix in- meaning.
M.instructions: The prefix in- can often mean 'not' or 'no'. However, in words such as invaluable, in- does not mean 'not' or 'no'. Choose YES if in- means 'not' or 'no', or NO if it doesn't.

questions.sentence: The **incoming** flight from Buenos Aires was delayed due to adverse weather conditions.
The gallery offered a generous **incentive** to staff who agreed to work extended hours during the festival.
A minor **incident** at the opening night ceremony received more media coverage than the exhibition itself.
Her **income** from selling original prints has grown remarkably over the past three years.
The critic’s remarks proved deeply **inflammatory** and sparked a very public dispute between the two artists.
The set designer came up with an **ingenious** solution to fit the entire backdrop onto the tiny stage.
The article contained several **inaccurate** claims about the painting’s provenance that needed to be corrected.
The rehearsal space was cramped and **inhospitable**, yet the cast worked there throughout the entire winter.
The level of funding allocated to the restoration project was frankly **insufficient** to cover even basic costs.
The lead actor was reportedly **intoxicated** when he arrived for the evening performance.
